Low-Level Voice Processing

Titles can use a handful of Xbox Media Objects (XMOs) to perform voice processing at a low level. In general, the need for this is quite rare. For the most part, titles can simply use the high-level voice processing engine instead.

Using XMOs your game can easily work with the headset and to encode and decode voice data. A general synopsis of low-level voice chat implementation follows:

  • Titles call the standard XGetDevices and other Input Device Functions to enumerate connected voice peripherals.
  • Once voice units are detected, create the voice device XMOs for the headphone and microphone using XVoiceCreateMediaObject.
  • The game code then feeds output to the headphones and retrieves input from the headset by supplying buffers to the XMO Process function, XMediaObject::Process. Titles implement a simple buffer queue and manage memory allocation for the buffers. Media are exchanged with the various XMOs through XMEDIAPACKET structures.
  • Games compress microphone packets intended for transmission over the network with the Voice Encoder XMO. Titles create this XMO using the XVoiceCreate family of encoder creation functions, and then send data to the encoder using the function, XMediaObject::Process. However, for efficiency IXVoiceEncoder::ProcessMultiple is recommended.
  • Incoming voice data from the network should be decoded using the Voice Decoder XMO. Titles create this XMO using the XVoiceCreate family of decoder creation functions, and then provide data to the decoder by calling XMediaObject::Process. Here again, IXVoiceDecoder::ProcessMultiple is recommended for efficiency. Alternatively, titles can use XVoiceQueueCreateMediaObject to simplify the queuing and timing requirements for packet decoding.
  • Decoded packets are sent to the headphone via the process function (XMediaObject::Process or IXVoiceDecoder::ProcessMultiple) on the headphone device XMO. Alternatively, mixing or voice effects can be applied at this stage via DirectSound.
  • Games deliver encoded microphone packets across the network using traditional WinSock function calls in the game's standard network layer. Likewise, incoming voice data is retrieved in this same network layer.

Developers should weigh the merits of a peer-to-peer arrangement for voice—whether or not ordinary game traffic is peer-to-peer—versus the increased packet overhead and bandwidth use of additional voice packets. Voice data will typically need to be sent to only a small number of current players, such as one's current team members or friends. Sending voice traffic as peer-to-peer among listeners saves obvious bandwidth. On the other hand, in games where everyone can hear you scream (to steal a quote), voice data should be piggybacked on existing game data traffic in order to save the costly overhead of Xbox secure packets.

Note    Low-level voice processing does not have access to the voicemail codecs. XHV is required in order to implement voice mail.

  • Creating and Using Voice Media Objects
  • Muting, Blocking, and Banning Players
  • Voice Effects, Masking, and Voice Recognition
